chunks.text: Michel M. Maharbiz is a Professor with the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science at the University of California, Berkeley. His research focuses on the extreme miniaturization of technology focused on building synthetic interfaces to cells and organisms. He is one of the inventors of 'neural dust', an ultrasonic interface for vanishingly small implants in the body. His group is also known for developing the world's first remotely radio-controlled cyborg beetles. This was named one of the top ten emerging technologies of 2009 by MIT's Technology Review (TR10) and was in Time Magazine's Top 50 Inventions of 2009.
chunks.question: What are Michel Maharbiz's main research interests?
chunks.answer: Extreme miniaturization and synthetic biological interfaces.

chunks.text: Prof. Maharbiz received his B.S. from Cornell University and his Ph.D. from the University of California, Berkeley under nanotechnologist Professor Roger T. Howe (EECS) and synthetic biologist Professor Jay D. Keasling (ChemE); his thesis work led to the foundation of Microreactor Technologies, Inc. which was acquired in 2009 by Pall Corporation. He is a Senior Member of the IEEE (Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society) and a member of the Society for Neuroscience.
chunks.question: Where did Michel Maharbiz earn his degrees?
chunks.answer: Cornell University BS and UC Berkeley PhD.

chunks.text: Prof. Maharbiz is a Chan-Zuckerberg (CZ) Biohub Investigator (2017), a Bakar Fellow (2014), recipient of a National Science Foundation CAREER Award (2009), a GE Scholar and an Intel IMAP Fellow. Michel's long term goal is understanding developmental mechanisms as a way to engineer and fabricate machines.
chunks.question: What is Michel Maharbiz's long-term research goal?
chunks.answer: Understanding developmental mechanisms to engineer machines.

chunks.text: Research Areas: Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO), Micro/Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS). Research Centers: Berkeley Sensor & Actuator Center (BSAC), Center for Neural Engineering & Prostheses (CNEP).
chunks.question: What are Michel Maharbiz's primary research areas?

chunks.text: Selected Honors and Awards: Institute of Electrical & Electronics Engineers (IEEE) Fellow (2026), McKnight Technological Innovations in Neuroscience Award (2017), Bakar Fellows Spark Award (2012), MIT Tech Review Top 10 Breakthrough Technologies (TR10) (2009), NSF Faculty Early Career Development Award (CAREER) (2009).
chunks.question: What major award did Michel Maharbiz receive in 2017?
chunks.answer: McKnight Technological Innovations in Neuroscience Award.
